does: This paper introduces Automated Progressive Red Teaming (APRT), a systematic framework for automatically generating adversarial prompts to uncover vulnerabilities in large language models. The approach consists of three coordinated modules: an Intention Expanding LLM that generates diverse initial attack samples, an Intention Hiding LLM that crafts deceptive prompts to evade safety mechanisms, and an Evil Maker that maintains prompt diversity while filtering out ineffective samples. These components work together across multiple rounds of interaction to progressively explore and exploit LLM vulnerabilities. The paper also proposes Attack Effectiveness Rate (AER) as a novel evaluation metric to better assess the success of red teaming attempts, addressing limitations in existing evaluation approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in automated red teaming and adversarial testing of large language models, extending beyond manual red teaming approaches that have proven effective but costly and unscalable. The paper responds to recognized gaps in existing automated red teaming frameworks, which lack explicit structure for framing red teaming as a learnable optimization task. It connects to prior work on adversarial prompt generation, safety evaluation of LLMs, and techniques for generating deceptive inputs that bypass alignment mechanisms. The research also engages with broader AI safety literature concerned with identifying and mitigating vulnerabilities in increasingly capable language models, and complements existing work on red teaming methodology and evaluation metrics.
enables: This framework enables more scalable and cost-effective identification of LLM vulnerabilities at scale, moving beyond the resource constraints of manual red teaming. The progressive, multi-round interaction approach establishes a foundation for iterative vulnerability discovery that could be extended to discover novel attack patterns and edge cases. The proposed Attack Effectiveness Rate metric provides a more rigorous evaluation standard for red teaming efforts, potentially enabling better comparison and benchmarking of safety testing approaches. This work could facilitate the development of more robust safety training procedures and defenses by providing systematic access to diverse failure modes. The learnable framework structure also opens avenues for applying machine learning techniques to optimize red teaming efficiency and could be adapted to emerging model architectures or safety challenges.
